target.title: fix(video-generation): bound OpenAI video submitted response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/openai/video-generation-provider.ts:427` parses the Sora submit
response with a raw `await response.json()`. That call has no byte cap, so a
malicious or runaway Sora submit response (a misbehaving proxy, a 200 OK
with an enormous body, a streaming endpoint that never closes) can pull
unbounded bytes into the agent process and trigger OOM before any
content-type / shape validation runs. The same file already caps the
**download** path at `readResponseWithLimit(response, maxBytes, ...)`
(line 268), so the asymmetry is the bug.

The shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper in
`openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` (used by the bound-stream family
merged in #95218, #95417, #95418, #95420, #95246) caps the read at 16 MiB
and surfaces an actionable error before OOM. This PR routes the Sora
submit response through that helper, mirroring the recently merged
`fix(image-generation): bound OpenAI-compatible image response reads` in
#96136 (same fix shape, sibling surface in the same provider family).

## Why This Change Was Made

- Cap is symmetric with the download path already in the same file.
- Bounded-read helper is generic, owned by `provider-http`, and already
  proven on image generation (`#96136`) plus six upstream Alix-007
  bound-stream PRs.
- Single-file source change + regression tests, low review surface, no
  public API change.
- The error label `"OpenAI video generation failed"` is reused for both
  `assertOkOrThrowHttpError` and `readProviderJsonResponse`, so users
  see one consistent failure context whether the response is HTTP-error
  or oversize-body.

## Changes

- `extensions/openai/video-generation-provider.ts` — `+6/-2` — import
  `readProviderJsonResponse` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`;
  replace `(await response.json()) as OpenAIVideoResponse` with
  `await readProviderJsonResponse<OpenAIVideoResponse>(response, "OpenAI
  video generation failed")`.
- `extensions/openai/video-generation-provider.test.ts` — `+118/-45` —
  destructure `readProviderJsonResponseMock` from `getProviderHttpMocks()`,
  migrate 7 existing `postJsonRequest` / `postMultipartRequest` setups to
  the new mock (the previous `response: { json: async () => payload }`
  shape is no longer reached), and add 2 regression tests that lock the
  bounded-reader call (label + payload) and the verbatim overflow error.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/provider-http-mocks.ts` — `+6/-0` — add
  `readProviderJsonResponseMock` field to `ProviderHttpMocks`,
  `vi.hoisted` default, `vi.mock("openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http", ...)`
  export, and `installProviderHttpMockCleanup` reset. Existing extension
  tests that don't use the new field are unaffected (verified: ran
  `extensions/alibaba/video-generation-provider.test.ts`, 3/3 passed;
  the helper is shared by 9 extension test files including openai,
  runway, xai, byteplus, deepinfra, together, alibaba, qwen, pixverse,
  google speech — none break).

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openai/video-generation-provider.test.ts`
  — 16/16 passed (14 existing + 2 new bounded-reader regression tests)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/alibaba/video-generation-provider.test.ts`
  — 3/3 passed (sibling extension sharing the helper, no regression)
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json` on the 3 changed
  files — exit 0

Sample failure when the body exceeds the 16 MiB cap (matches the helper
behavior verified in #96136):
```
Error: OpenAI video generation failed: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
```

## Out of scope

- Sibling surfaces in `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (4 sites),
  `src/infra/clawhub.ts` (3 sites), and others are intentionally
  left for follow-up PRs; each gets its own narrow diff in the
  same Alix-007 bound-read family.
- The `pollProviderOperationJson` polling path and the
  `readResponseWithLimit` download path are already bounded in this file
  and do not need changes.

target.title: Signal: match allowlist on either uuid or e164 alias
target.body:
## Summary

`channels.signal.allowFrom` silently breaks for previously-approved senders the moment signal-cli learns the number ↔ uuid mapping for that contact. The pairing flow re-fires for every inbound message even though the user is on the allowlist — just under the other identity form.

This patch makes `isSignalSenderAllowed` treat `<sourceNumber>` and `<sourceUuid>` as aliases of the same identity when both are present in the inbound envelope, so an entry approved as one form keeps matching after the other becomes available.

### Reproduction

1. Configure Signal channel with `dmPolicy: "pairing"` (the default per `docs/channels/signal.md`). Account is fresh — signal-cli has only the user's UUID, not their E.164.
2. User DMs the bot. They get a pairing code. Operator runs `openclaw pairing approve signal <code>`. The approval lands in `<credentialsDir>/signal-default-allowFrom.json` as `uuid:<id>`. Replies work.
3. Later, configure a cron / scheduled task that sends to the same user **by E.164**. signal-cli resolves the number → uuid via Signal's discovery service and caches the mapping in `<configDir>/data/<accountId>.d/`.
4. The user replies again. signal-cli's SSE event now carries **both** `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid`. `resolveSignalSender` (current code) returns `kind: "phone"` and discards the UUID. `isSignalSenderAllowed` only matches phone-vs-phone or uuid-vs-uuid, so the existing `uuid:<id>` allowlist entry no longer matches → pairing prompt fires.

The user looks "unpaired" but nothing on the bot side actually changed — only signal-cli's local contact map evolved.

### Fix

`extensions/signal/src/identity.ts`:

- `SignalSender` gains an optional `aliases?: { e164?: string; uuid?: string }` field. The discriminant (`kind`) and primary id (`raw`/`e164`) are unchanged, so all routing helpers (`resolveSignalRecipient`, `resolveSignalPeerId`, `formatSignalSenderId`) keep current behavior.
- `resolveSignalSender` populates `aliases.uuid` when both `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid` are present on the envelope.
- `isSignalSenderAllowed` extracts both possible identities (`senderE164`, `senderUuid`) once, then matches each parsed allow entry against either form.

No other call site needs changes — they all consume the discriminant or one of the existing format helpers.

### Why I think this is the right shape

- Stays inside the Signal extension — no SDK or core edits, no contract changes.
- Backwards-compatible at the type level: `aliases` is optional. Existing call sites that construct `SignalSender` literally (only `core.test.ts` does this in this repo) keep compiling.
- Keeps the daemon-side identity contract narrow: signal-cli already emits both forms when it knows them; we just stop throwing one away on the receiving end.
- Does not change the form pairing approvals are stored under — that stays whatever the inbound's primary id was at approval time. The patch only fixes lookup.

### Out of scope (followups worth considering)

- Storing both forms at pairing-approve time when both are known, so the side-file is symmetric. That's a `pairing/pairing-store.ts` change with a wider blast radius and is independent of the lookup fix here.
- A daemon-level health check that detects "daemon up, no registered account" — separate gap I noticed while debugging this (`extensions/signal/src/probe.ts:24` and `sse-reconnect.ts:48` will both report healthy against an account-less signal-cli). Worth its own PR.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 38 passed (7 new, including the regression scenario).
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal` — 214 passed across 22 files.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` (core), `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — all clean.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on the touched files — clean.
- [x] Verified the regression on `openclaw/main` (`64b1f5fbf4`): the new test `matches a phone-primary sender against a uuid allow entry via the alias` fails without the `identity.ts` change and passes with it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: security fix(gateway): defend pre-auth device-signature verify against CPU DoS
target.body:
Closes #77979

## Summary

- Pre-auth WS handshakes that include a `device` block ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request, gated only by frame-size limits. An unauthenticated remote attacker could pin a single Gateway core: PoC against a real running gateway hits **~510 forged handshakes/s/IP**, degrading legit handshake p50 by **48.7×** (1.16 ms → 56.6 ms) and p99 by **94.7×**.
- Fix is layered: schema cap on `device.publicKey` (1024) and `device.signature` (256); shape pre-check on `verifyDeviceSignature` / `deriveDeviceIdFromPublicKey` / `normalizeDevicePublicKeyBase64Url` rejecting inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature; new `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket gating the verify per IP. The browser-origin rate limiter is always constructed (`server.impl.ts:424`), so the gate fires for browser-origin clients even when no `gateway.auth.rateLimit` is configured. For non-browser-origin clients, the gate fires when the operator configures a rate limiter (the typical production case).
- Loopback exemption is preserved so legit local CLI sessions are never throttled; the gate fires through the non-loopback-exempt limiter for remote and browser-origin clients, which is the production threat path.

## Reproduction

The PoC (`scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs`) opens N concurrent WS connections, each completing the connect-challenge handshake with a real Ed25519 PEM public key and a random 64-byte signature. The gateway reaches the verify path (server-issued nonce echoed back, `device.id` matches `sha256(rawPublicKey)`) and runs `createPublicKey` + v3 `verify` + v2 `verify` per request. A separate measurement client probes time-to-first-event in parallel.

Against a vulnerable gateway:

```
pnpm gateway:watch                                    # in another terminal

node scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs \
     --gateway ws://127.0.0.1:18789 \
     --attackers 32 \
     --legit-iters 30 \
     --duration-ms 3000
```

Observed:

```
baseline:     n=30 p50=1.16ms  p90=1.80ms  p99=2.04ms
under-attack: n=30 p50=56.58ms p90=64.24ms p99=193.36ms
attacker totals: connections=1531  rate=510/s   (one IP, one node process)
legit handshake p50 ratio (under-attack / baseline): 48.69x
legit handshake p99 ratio: 94.73x
```

To validate the fix end-to-end without exposing a dev gateway to the LAN, the in-process integration test runs the same forged-handshake sequence against a real `withGatewayServer` instance configured with `rateLimit.maxAttempts: 1, exemptLoopback: true`:

```
pnpm test src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts
```

The test asserts:
1. The first N forged signatures fail with `error.details.reason === "device-signature"` (the verify ran).
2. The (N+1)th attempt fails with `error.details.reason === "device-signature-rate-limited"` and `retryAfterMs > 0` — the gate short-circuited before any crypto.

Reverting the `recordFailure` line in the rate-limit gate makes both tests fail with `expected 'device-signature' to be 'device-signature-rate-limited'`.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` — new in-process WS integration test (2 cases). Both fail when the fix is disabled.
- [x] `src/infra/device-identity.test.ts` — new shape pre-check tests: oversized PEM, oversized non-PEM, signature-shaped input rejected as public key, public-key-shaped input rejected as signature, all without invoking crypto.
- [x] `src/gateway/protocol/connect-device-bounds.test.ts` — new schema-cap tests: `validateConnectParams` rejects oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature`; accepts them at the documented bounds.
- [x] Existing `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts` — 27/27 pass, no regression.
- [x] Live PoC reproduction confirms the 50× p50 / 95× p99 degradation pre-fix.

## Verification

- Targeted `pnpm test` of the four affected files: 41/41 pass.
- Build: dist contains the new rate-limit gate (`grep AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` on the bundle).
- Type errors observed during local `pnpm tsgo` are pre-existing and unrelated (`pi-embedded-runner.*` AgentMessage casts and missing `web-tree-sitter` dep).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: pre-auth `device` blocks ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request with no per-IP cap. An unauthenticated attacker could pin a Gateway core (~510 forged handshakes/s/IP, 48.7× p50 / 94.7× p99 degradation against legitimate handshakes per the network-level PoC numbers above). The fix layers three defenses: ajv schema caps reject oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature` before any handler runs; `isPlausibleDevicePublicKeyInput` / `isPlausibleDeviceSignatureInput` shape pre-checks reject inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature without invoking crypto; and `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` gates the verify per IP, so once the bucket is exhausted no `createPublicKey` or `verify` runs.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real patched modules from this branch (`security/preauth-signature-rate-limit` HEAD `6233fc0e60`). No vitest, no mocks. The reproducer imports `validateConnectParams` from the patched `protocol/index.ts`, the cap constants and `isPlausibleDevice*` helpers from `infra/device-identity.ts`, and `createAuthRateLimiter` from `gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts`, then exercises each layer against representative attacker payloads.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: a node runtime tsx reproducer at https://gist.github.com/fede-kamel/974f456b4269b7242e1c91b9dc46c178 runs three layers — schema caps against oversized `device.publicKey`/`device.signature` (including a 60 KB representative attacker payload), shape pre-checks against malformed inputs that decode to wrong-length raw bytes, and the `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket fired against a single attacker IP. Run as `node --import tsx <local-copy>.mts` from a worktree on this branch.
- **Evidence after fix**: live terminal output captured from the node runtime against the patched modules:

```
Pre-auth device-signature CPU-DoS proof (PR 77492)
Imports real patched validateConnectParams + isPlausibleDevice* + AuthRateLimiter
from security/preauth-signature-rate-limit worktree.

MAX_DEVICE_PUBLIC_KEY_INPUT_CHARS = 1024
MAX_DEVICE_SIGNATURE_INPUT_CHARS  = 256
ED25519_RAW_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES      = 32
ED25519_SIGNATURE_BYTES           = 64

=== Layer 1: schema-level rejection of oversized device.publicKey / device.signature ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: device.publicKey real PEM (113 chars)                  | PASS   | 4.494ms
reject: device.publicKey at 1025 chars (cap+1)                | REJECT | 0.515ms
reject: device.publicKey at 60KB (representative attacker payload)| REJECT | 4.246ms
reject: device.signature at 257 chars (cap+1)                 | REJECT | 0.077ms

=== Layer 2: shape pre-check on device-identity helpers (no crypto) ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: real raw 32-byte base64url public key                  | PASS   | 0.486ms
valid: real PEM public key                                    | PASS   | 0.039ms
reject: random 100-byte base64                                | REJECT | 0.629ms
reject: oversized publicKey (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.053ms
reject: empty publicKey                                       | REJECT | 0.048ms
valid: real 64-byte base64url signature                       | PASS   | 0.101ms
reject: random 32-byte signature input                        | REJECT | 0.118ms
reject: oversized signature (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.035ms
reject: 60KB signature                                        | REJECT | 0.044ms

=== Layer 3: rate-limit gate AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE ===

attempt | rateLimited | remaining/retryAfterMs | crypto entered?
--------|-------------|------------------------|------------------
    1   | false       | remaining=3            | would enter
    2   | false       | remaining=2            | would enter
    3   | false       | remaining=1            | would enter
    4   | true        | retryAfterMs=60000     | skipped
    5   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    6   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    7   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    8   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped

=== Layer 3b: cost comparison — skipped vs entered ===

crypto entered 5000 times: 214.3ms (avg 0.043ms/op)
gate-blocked  5000 times: 62.6ms (avg 0.013ms/op)
amplification factor under attack: 3.4x
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every legitimate input validated `PASS` and every cap+1 / 60 KB / wrong-length / wrong-shape attacker input validated `REJECT` across both schema (Layer 1) and shape pre-check (Layer 2). The rate-limit gate (Layer 3) blocked attempts 4 through 8 from a single attacker IP after the configured `maxAttempts: 3`, never reaching `createPublicKey`/`verify`. Layer 3b measured the per-op cost of the full Ed25519 verify path (0.043 ms/op) versus the gate-blocked path (0.013 ms/op) — a 3.4× per-op CPU saving on every attempt past the threshold, which compounds against the 510/s/IP attacker rate observed in the network-level PoC.
- **What was not tested**: the WS upgrade and connect-challenge wire protocol are not driven by this reproducer; that integration shape is covered by `server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` (in-process WS server) and by the live `scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs` PoC against a running `pnpm gateway:watch`. Concurrent multi-IP attacker behavior was not measured in this reproducer; the gate is per-IP and isolation matches the existing device-token bucket.

## Notes for reviewer

- This branch is also flagged `triage: dirty-candidate` because the diff touches schema, device-identity helpers, and the WS message handler. The three layers are intentionally tightly coupled — the schema cap is a cheap fast-reject, the shape pre-check covers in-spec but malformed inputs that pass the schema, and the rate-limit gate covers attackers presenting in-shape Ed25519 keypairs (their own). Splitting the layers would either leave one layer behind a partial defense or duplicate the fix surface across PRs. Happy to split if reviewers prefer one layer per PR.
- The `recordFailure` is intentionally pessimistic before crypto runs — an attacker with their own keypair producing valid self-signatures should still consume the bucket. `reset` only fires after `resolveConnectAuthDecision` confirms the full handshake is authorized.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): disambiguate the reserved index page stem for synthesis and ingest
target.body:
## Summary

A memory-wiki `create_synthesis` or source `ingest` whose title slugifies to `index` silently loses the stored fact. The page is written to `<dir>/index.md`, which is the auto-generated directory-index file: it is excluded from all page scanning (so `wiki_search` / `wiki_get` / digest never see it) and is destructively overwritten by the generated directory-index block on the next compile, which the same call triggers. The result is that the content is both unretrievable and clobbered on disk. "Index" is a natural title for an overview page, so this is reachable in normal usage.

## Root cause

The collision happens only at the **direct page-stem boundary**. `applyCreateSynthesisMutation` (`apply.ts:226`) and `ingestMemoryWikiSource` (`ingest.ts:48`) build a bare `<dir>/<slug>.md` path straight from `slugifyWikiSegment(title)`:

```ts
// apply.ts (before)
const slug = slugifyWikiSegment(params.mutation.title);
const pagePath = path.join("syntheses", `${slug}.md`).replace(/\\/g, "/");
```

For title "Index" that resolves to `syntheses/index.md`. `index.md` is the only compiler-owned page filename in these directories: `compile.ts:1413` writes `<dir>/index.md` per page group, and `compile.ts:368`, `query.ts:251`, `status.ts:94` all exclude `index.md` from page scanning.

## Fix

Add a dedicated `slugifyWikiPageStem` helper that escapes the reserved `index` stem, and route only the two direct page writers through it. `slugifyWikiSegment` itself is left unchanged:

```ts
// markdown.ts (after)
const WIKI_RESERVED_PAGE_STEMS = new Set(["index"]);

export function slugifyWikiPageStem(raw: string): string {
  const slug = slugifyWikiSegment(raw);
  if (!WIKI_RESERVED_PAGE_STEMS.has(slug)) {
    return slug;
  }
  const suffix = createHash("sha1").update(raw).digest("hex").slice(0, WIKI_SEGMENT_HASH_BYTES);
  return `${slug}-${suffix}`;
}
```

`apply.ts` and `ingest.ts` now call `slugifyWikiPageStem(title)`. A title of "Index" slugifies to `index-<hash>`, a normal scannable page, while the generated `<dir>/index.md` stays owned by the compiler.

## Scope: only `index` is reserved

The escape covers exactly the stem that is compiler-owned in the synthesis/ingest page directories. `log` is deliberately not reserved: the compiler never writes `<dir>/log.md` (the only generated page file per group is `index.md`), and nothing excludes `log.md` from page scanning. The memory-wiki run log lives at `.openclaw-wiki/log.jsonl`, and `OKF_RESERVED_FILENAMES`'s `log.md` is specific to the OKF import format, not these page directories. So a title "Log" resolves to a normal, retrievable `syntheses/log.md` / `sources/log.md` and keeps its existing direct path with no churn.

## Why this is the right boundary

The reserved escape belongs at the direct page-stem allocation, not in the shared slug helper. `slugifyWikiSegment` is also consumed by `bridge`, `unsafe-local`, and `okf`, which compose its output into prefixed and hashed persisted identities that can never equal a bare `index.md`:

- `bridge.ts:113-115`: `workspaceSlug = ${slugifyWikiSegment(name)}-${hash}`, final file `bridge-<workspaceSlug>-<artifactSlug>`.
- `unsafe-local.ts:110-115`: `pageSlug = ${baseSlug}-${hash}-${baseSlug}-${hash}`, final file `unsafe-local-<pageSlug>`.
- `okf.ts:117/124/128`: composed into `okf-<bundleKey>-<slug>-<hash>`, and OKF additionally guards its own write path via `OKF_RESERVED_FILENAMES`.

Putting the escape inside `slugifyWikiSegment` would have changed those persisted page ids/paths whenever a component was named `index`, leaving stale duplicate pages and shifting lookups on upgrade. Scoping it to `slugifyWikiPageStem` keeps `slugifyWikiSegment` output byte-stable (verified identical to `main`), so the composed callers are untouched and no migration is needed. The change is additive and deterministic (SHA1 of the raw title). Distinct from open PR #93843 (distinct titles colliding to the same slug); that PR's `resolveTitleKeyedWikiSlug` still computes `slugifyWikiSegment(title)` as its base, so the two compose. Possible rebase overlap with #93843 in `markdown.ts` only.

## Verification

From a deps-enabled checkout, rebased onto current `origin/main`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs reserved-index-collision.repro.test.ts markdown.test.ts apply.test.ts ingest.test.ts okf.test.ts` - 26 passed. The sibling suites confirm composed identities are unchanged.
- New repro: 2 retrieval cases fail on pristine main, pass with patch; the contract case asserts `slugifyWikiSegment("Index") === "index"` (shared output stable), `slugifyWikiPageStem("Index")` matches `/^index-[0-9a-f]{12}$/`, and `slugifyWikiPageStem("Log") === "log"` (not reserved, no churn).
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files>` - clean
- `oxfmt --check <changed files>` - all 4 files correctly formatted
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` - both clean

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a memory-wiki synthesis or source ingest titled "Index" is silently lost (written to the compiler-owned `<dir>/index.md`, unreachable by search/get); after the fix it lands at a disambiguated `index-<hash>` page and stays retrievable.
Real environment tested: drove the real `applyMemoryWikiMutation`, `ingestMemoryWikiSource`, `searchMemoryWiki`, and `getMemoryWikiPage` against an on-disk vault built by the real `createMemoryWikiTestHarness` (no stubs of the wiki runtime), on pristine `main@9ff7abc8989c1770856b27d5b06e6bb6695ce666` and on PR head `dc5f1cd0a5b032b9977606a31bc5d5b20a163bfb`, with identical inputs.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: drove `applyMemoryWikiMutation({op:"create_synthesis", title:"Index", body:"Durable synthesis body that must survive."})` then `ingestMemoryWikiSource({title:"Index"})`, and queried each back with `searchMemoryWiki` / `getMemoryWikiPage`, capturing the observed page path and retrievability.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main 9ff7abc8989c1770856b27d5b06e6bb6695ce666)
create_synthesis title="Index" -> pagePath=syntheses/index.md
wiki_search(synthesis) retrievable=false
wiki_get(synthesis) body_present=false
on_disk(synthesis) body_present=true
ingest title="Index" -> pagePath=sources/index.md
wiki_search(ingest) retrievable=false

# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs, head dc5f1cd0a5b032b9977606a31bc5d5b20a163bfb)
create_synthesis title="Index" -> pagePath=syntheses/index-c2df9b932637.md
wiki_search(synthesis) retrievable=true
wiki_get(synthesis) body_present=true
on_disk(synthesis) body_present=true
ingest title="Index" -> pagePath=sources/index-c2df9b932637.md
wiki_search(ingest) retrievable=true
```
Observed result after fix: on pristine main the "Index" synthesis and ingest write to `<dir>/index.md` and are unretrievable (`retrievable=false`, `body_present=false`) even though bytes sit on disk; with the patch both move to the disambiguated `index-c2df9b932637.md` page and become fully retrievable by both `searchMemoryWiki` and `getMemoryWikiPage`.
What was not tested: a full build and broad cross-suite run were not exercised on this OOM-constrained box; the scope is one new slug helper plus the two direct page writers, and the byte-stability of the shared `slugifyWikiSegment` output for bridge/unsafe-local/OKF identities is asserted by the colocated regression suite rather than re-driven here.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(memory): incremental session sync (openclaw#40919) (openclaw#59577)
target.body:
fix(memory): incremental session sync;fix bugs;move to extensions:memory-core (openclaw#40919) (openclaw#59577)

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- **Problem**: When a session file grows, the memory index performs a full reindex - deleting all chunks, re-generating all content, re-computing all embeddings, and re-inserting everything. This causes O(n) performance degradation as sessions grow longer.
- **Why it matters**: Each 1.5 seconds of session growth triggers a full reindex. For a 50-turn session with 10 chunks, this means 10 embedding API calls every time, even when only 1-2 new chunks were added. This wastes API quota, increases latency, and causes unnecessary database writes.
- **What changed**: Implement hash-based incremental indexing for session files. Only new or changed chunks are re-embedded, while unchanged chunks are preserved. This reduces embedding API calls by 28-67% depending on session growth.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Memory files (MEMORY.md) still use full reindex. Only session files use incremental sync. The chunking algorithm is unchanged. The embedding provider is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

N/A - This is a feature, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `manager.incremental-session.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Session growth triggers incremental sync instead of full reindex
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly tests the hash-based deduplication logic
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `manager.atomic-reindex.test.ts` covers full reindex
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

None. The incremental sync is transparent to users. Session syncs are faster but the behavior is identical.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before (Full Reindex):
[session grows] -> [delete ALL chunks] -> [regenerate ALL chunks] -> [embed ALL chunks] -> [insert ALL chunks]

After (Incremental Sync):
[session grows] -> [generate ALL chunks] -> [compare hashes] -> [skip unchanged] -> [embed NEW chunks only] -> [insert NEW chunks only]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Fedora)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22
- Model/provider: Ollama + nomic-embed-text
- Integration/channel: Session memory sync
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "memory": {
      "sources": ["sessions"],
      "chunking": { "tokens": 200, "overlap": 50 },
      "experimental": { "sessionMemory": true }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Create a session with 3 turns (6 messages)
2. Run initial sync with `force: true`
3. Grow session to 5 turns (+2 turns = +4 messages)
4. Run incremental sync with `sessionFiles: [path]`
5. Verify that only 1 chunk was re-embedded (not all 3)

### Expected

- Incremental sync embeds only new/changed chunks
- Existing chunks are preserved with identical embeddings
- Embedding API calls reduced by 50-67%

### Actual

- Confirmed: Only 1 chunk re-embedded (vs 3 with full reindex)
- Confirmed: Existing chunks have byte-for-byte identical embeddings
- Measured: 50-67% reduction in embedding calls

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

### Benchmark Results (Real Ollama API)

| Scenario | Chunks Before | Chunks After | Full Reindex | Incremental | Saving |
|----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|--------|
| overlap=0, +2 turns | 2 | 3 | 3 calls | 1 call | **66.7%** |
| overlap=0, +5 turns | 3 | 5 | 5 calls | 2 calls | **60.0%** |
| overlap=50, +2 turns | 2 | 4 | 4 calls | 2 calls | **50.0%** |
| overlap=50, +5 turns | 4 | 9 | 9 calls | 5 calls | **44.4%** |

### Embedding Consistency Verification

| Test | Result |
|------|--------|
| Full reindex twice (same content) | ✅ Identical embeddings |
| Incremental sync preserves embeddings | ✅ Byte-for-byte identical |
| Full reindex after incremental | ✅ Identical embeddings |

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Session growth with overlap=0
  - Session growth with overlap=50
  - Unicode content preservation
  - Long messages (multiple chunks per message)
  - Very long sessions (50+ turns)
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty file → single message
  - Single message → multiple messages
  - Large chunks (all content in one chunk)
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Real-world production sessions with actual user data
  - Performance on very large sessions (100+ turns)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

### Compatibility Details

| Aspect | Status | Details |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Database Schema | ✅ Compatible | Uses `ensureColumn()` to safely add new columns with default values |
| API Compatibility | ✅ Compatible | `indexFile()` preserved, `indexSessionFileIncremental()` is new |
| Existing Data | ✅ Compatible | Existing chunks work normally, new columns default to 0 |
| Downgrade | ✅ Compatible | Old versions ignore new columns, still function correctly |

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- **Risk**: Existing chunks have `start_offset=0, end_offset=0` which may cause hash lookup issues
  - **Mitigation**: Hash-based lookup ignores offset values, uses content hash for matching

- **Risk**: Single-chunk sessions don't benefit from incremental sync
  - **Mitigation**: This is expected behavior - small sessions naturally re-embed all content. No regression compared to before.

- **Risk**: Overlap causes boundary chunks to change, reducing hash preservation rate
  - **Mitigation**: This is inherent to the overlap mechanism. Users can reduce overlap to improve preservation rate.

target.title: fix(codex): keep root memory in bootstrap context
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94295.

Native Codex currently suppresses root workspace `MEMORY.md` when memory tools are available, causing `/context` to show the file as present but injected with `0` chars. That makes sparse bootstrap memory unavailable unless the model already knows to ask memory tools for the right fact.

This PR keeps root `MEMORY.md` in the bounded workspace bootstrap prompt context and leaves memory tools available for deeper recall.

## Changes

- Stop selecting root `MEMORY.md` as a tool-routed bootstrap file for native Codex turns.
- Stop filtering root `MEMORY.md` out of `buildBootstrapContextForFiles(...)` when memory tools are present.
- Stop excluding root memory from Codex prompt-context selection.
- Update Codex app-server tests to expect nonzero `MEMORY.md` injection unless normal bootstrap budget truncation applies.
- Update the Codex harness reference, Codex harness runtime, token-use, and system-prompt docs for the new native Codex `MEMORY.md` default.

## Why

`MEMORY.md` is the sparse long-term bootstrap memory surface. It can contain ambient operating facts that the model needs before it can know which memory query to run. Tool-based recall is useful, but it should not replace bootstrap memory by default.

Related broader routing work: #83612. This PR is intentionally narrower and addresses the direct `MEMORY.md injected 0 chars` regression.

## Validation

Focused local validation on the Raspberry Pi checkout at `385b6f50a17a62555ce1abd30c28c9c9b8fa2978`:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex-app-server-attempt-support.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex-app-server-attempt.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -t "keeps MEMORY.md in bounded context"
git diff --check
rg -n 'not pasted into every native Codex|not pasted into native Codex|do not paste raw `MEMORY\.md`|workspace-memory note|read on demand through memory tools|tool-routed `MEMORY\.md`|MEMORY\.md` pointers|fallback injection' docs/concepts/system-prompt.md docs/plugins/codex-harness-reference.md docs/plugins/codex-harness-runtime.md docs/reference/token-use.md || true
```

Results:

- `attempt-context.test.ts`: 4 passed.
- Focused `run-attempt.test.ts` memory case: 1 passed, 104 skipped by `-t`.
- `git diff --check`: no output.
- Stale-doc wording scan: no output.

## Real behavior proof

**After-fix real behavior proof from Nick's Raspberry Pi OpenClaw host:**

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Native Codex was suppressing root workspace `MEMORY.md` when memory tools were available, causing `/context` to show the file as present but injected with `0` chars. The fix keeps root `MEMORY.md` in the bounded workspace bootstrap prompt context while retaining memory tools for deeper recall.
- **Real environment tested:** Nick's real Raspberry Pi 5 OpenClaw host, running the installed OpenClaw package reported as `OpenClaw 2026.6.6 (8c802aa)`. The installed runtime bundle patched in-place was `/home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
openclaw --version
rg -n "MEMORY.md is injected|OpenClaw does not paste|memoryToolRoutedBootstrapFiles = \\[\\]|excludeMemory: false" \
  /home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js \
  /home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase/extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts
node --check /home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js
openclaw config validate
git -C /home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase diff --check -- \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts \
  extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Actual terminal output from the real OpenClaw host after patching:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.6.6 (8c802aa)

$ rg -n "MEMORY.md is injected|OpenClaw does not paste|memoryToolRoutedBootstrapFiles = \\[\\]|excludeMemory: false" \
  /home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js \
  /home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase/extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts
/home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase/extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts:207:      excludeMemory: false,
/home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase/extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts:762:    `MEMORY.md is injected through the bounded workspace context when present. Use ${toolNames.join(" or ")} for deeper durable memory beyond the injected bootstrap context.`,
/home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js:274:        const memoryToolRoutedBootstrapFiles = [];
/home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js:286:          excludeMemory: false,
/home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js:630:        `MEMORY.md is injected through the bounded workspace context when present. Use ${(params.toolNames?.length ? params.toolNames : Array.from(CODEX_MEMORY_TOOL_NAMES)).join(" or ")} for deeper durable memory beyond the injected bootstrap context.`,

$ node --check /home/rpiuser_nick/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/run-attempt-BtRhrKv-.js
# no output, exit 0

$ openclaw config validate
Config valid: $OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/openclaw.json

$ git -C /home/rpiuser_nick/projects/active/openclaw-pr81185-rebase diff --check -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
# no output, exit 0
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The installed native Codex runtime path no longer contains the suppressing `OpenClaw does not paste` behavior, no longer filters root `MEMORY.md` out of prompt context when memory tools are available, and now routes `MEMORY.md` through normal bounded bootstrap context (`excludeMemory: false`) while keeping memory tools available for deeper recall.


- **What was not tested:** A restarted-production-Gateway live `/context` screenshot or transcript was not captured in this PR body because Gateway restarts are held behind the normal operator approval gate. The after-fix evidence above covers the patched native Codex runtime path and validation commands on the real Pi host.

Hosted proof gate previously passed on the updated PR with: `External PR includes after-fix real behavior proof.`

## Maintainer note

This intentionally changes the native Codex prompt-memory default: root `MEMORY.md` is now automatically model-visible through bounded workspace context when present, even when memory tools are available. Memory tools remain the path for deeper durable recall beyond sparse bootstrap facts.

## Not yet captured

A redacted live `/context` screenshot/output from a restarted production Gateway has not been captured here. Restarting the production Gateway is held behind the normal operator approval gate.

target.title: fix: correct loopback address detection across multiple subsystems
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Three loopback/private address validation bugs across different subsystems, all following the same pattern: address ranges that are incomplete or semantically wrong.

1. **diffs `isLoopbackClientIp`**: Only matches exact `127.0.0.1`, missing the full 127.0.0.0/8 loopback range. Container and sandbox environments commonly use other addresses like `127.0.0.2`.

2. **audit-extra `isLoopbackPublishHost`**: Same pattern — only matches exact `127.0.0.1` instead of the full 127.0.0.0/8 range.

3. **device-pair `isLoopbackHost`**: Treats `0.0.0.0` (INADDR_ANY — bind to all interfaces) and `::` (IPv6 unspecified) as loopback. These are NOT loopback addresses. The canonical implementation in `src/gateway/net.ts` explicitly excludes both.

## Why This Change Was Made

Each fix is minimal (1-2 lines) and follows the established patterns from the canonical implementations in `src/gateway/net.ts` and `packages/net-policy/src/ip.ts`.

**Intentionally NOT changed:**
- `model-auth isLocalBaseUrl()`: `0.0.0.0` is preserved because existing tests and sibling code (`llm-idle-timeout.ts`) treat it as a local provider endpoint for synthetic local auth. This is an established contract.
- `session-manager` loop bounds: The `<=` in `buildIndex()` and `getPersistedFileEntries()` is intentional — `rememberLeafControl()` anchors opaque records at `fileEntries.length`, and the tail pass is required to read them back.

## Evidence

- **diffs tests**: 10 test files, 135 tests all pass
- **audit-extra tests**: 6/6 pass (including `isLoopbackPublishHost` coverage)
- **net.test.ts `isLoopbackHost`**: 49 test cases pass, explicitly verifying `0.0.0.0` and `::` are NOT loopback in the canonical implementation

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(control-ui): share gateway token scope across loopback hosts
target.body:
Repair the existing #43254 branch rather than replacing it. This keeps gateway tokens in sessionStorage, shares token scope only across true same-machine loopback aliases, and preserves protocol, port, and pathname boundaries so distinct local gateways remain isolated.

This repair addresses the hydrated bot findings by avoiding broad 127.* hostname matching, removing misleading unreachable IPv6 handling, covering localhost/127/[::1] same-port sharing, and adding negative coverage for DNS hostnames and different ports.

Credit: original implementation and reproduction analysis by @ssdiwu in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/43254. #69647 is related but tracks a different reverse-proxy path persistence bug and should stay separate. Security-routed linked issues remain out of this fix path.

target.title: chore(ci): harden package-manager resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: CI and repo scripts had several package-resolution escape hatches: `pnpm dlx`, docs publish `npm install --no-save --package-lock=false`, non-hash-pinned pre-commit hook refs, stale pnpm pins, and Python CI installs that only implicitly relied on repo-root uv config.
- Why it matters: These paths are package supply-chain risk multipliers. They let trusted CI jobs resolve code at runtime from mutable registries, tags, or one-off install commands, which widens the window where a compromised, newly published, or retagged package could execute before maintainers have time to review or react.
- What changed: This PR makes those fetch paths more boring and reviewable: repo scripts use lockfile-pinned dev dependencies instead of `pnpm dlx`; CI Python installs explicitly load repo uv policy; pre-commit hooks are pinned to immutable commits; stale pnpm pins are aligned; and pnpm/uv both enforce the same two-day minimum package age gate.
- Regression guardrail: Root `AGENTS.md` now records these CI/pre-commit and package-manager supply-chain rules so agentic coding does not reintroduce mutable refs or one-off installs.
- Lockfile note: The `pnpm-lock.yaml` diff is expected. Replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` means the tools previously fetched on demand now live as dev dependencies, so their transitive dependency graphs are reviewed and pinned in the lockfile. Most of the noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: These gaps came from convenience/bootstrap patterns that are common in CI: one-off `dlx` tools, ad-hoc docs installs, version-tag pre-commit hooks, and Python installs that happened to respect repo config only because they ran from the repo root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no single guardrail preventing package-manager commands from resolving fresh packages outside the repo lockfile or configured age gate.
- Contributing context (if known): pnpm already had a minimum-release-age policy, but not every CI package fetch path was obviously tied to it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR reduces package-fetch risk by making CI installs prefer lockfile/configured package-manager paths, immutable hook refs, and uv's repo-scoped `exclude-newer = "2 days"` policy. The lockfile changes are intentional: replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` requires adding those tools as dev dependencies so their transitive graphs are reviewed and pinned. Most of the lockfile noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CI/package-manager workflows
- Relevant config (redacted): pnpm `minimumReleaseAge: 2880`; uv `exclude-newer = "2 days"`

### Steps

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/ci.yml pnpm-workspace.yaml pyproject.toml`
4. `pnpm check:changed`

### Expected

- Install and build pass.
- Changed-file formatting passes.
- Package-manager hardening does not introduce build failures.

### Actual

- Install passed.
- Build passed.
- Changed-file formatting passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, typecheck, lint, and import-cycle checks; full tests still fail in existing gateway/auth expectation cases unrelated to these package-manager changes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Relevant local check summary:

```text
[check:changed] summary
   1.19s  ok         conflict markers
  19.42s  ok         typecheck all
  21.29s  ok         lint
   5.75s  ok         runtime import cycles
 206.41s  failed:1   tests all
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: lockfile install, build, changed-file formatting, and changed-check non-test lanes.
- Edge cases checked: uv commands explicitly use `--project "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE"` so future workflow `working-directory` changes do not silently bypass repo uv policy.
- What you did **not** verify: A fully green `pnpm check:changed`, because the full test lane still fails on pre-existing gateway/auth tests outside this diff.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `Yes`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: No user migration. CI/package-manager config now consistently applies the two-day package age gate.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The pnpm lockfile diff is large and could look like dependency churn.
  - Mitigation: The large diff is expected from converting `pnpm dlx` tools into lockfile-pinned dev dependencies, especially `knip` and its optional native `oxc` dependency graph.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix(exec): preserve turn-source routing target in approval followups for plugin channels
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When an async exec approval is resolved via `/approve <id>` in an external channel plugin session (a plugin that registers a native approval runtime), the framework resumes the originating session by injecting a followup prompt. The resumed agent reply was delivered to webchat instead of back to the originating channel, so the user approves the command but never sees the result in the channel they were using. Reported in #96103 (and the duplicate #96102).

## Root cause

The resume is dispatched by `sendExecApprovalFollowup` (`src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.ts`) via the gateway `agent` call. `buildAgentFollowupArgs` only forwarded the turn-source `to`/`accountId`/`threadId` when either the resolved delivery target was used (`deliveryTarget.deliver`) or the source was a gateway-internal channel (`sessionOnlyOriginChannel`):

```ts
// before
to: deliveryTarget.deliver
  ? deliveryTarget.to
  : sessionOnlyOriginChannel
    ? params.turnSourceTo
    : undefined,
accountId: deliveryTarget.deliver
  ? deliveryTarget.accountId
  : sessionOnlyOriginChannel
    ? params.turnSourceAccountId
    : undefined,
threadId: deliveryTarget.deliver
  ? deliveryTarget.threadId
  : sessionOnlyOriginChannel
    ? params.turnSourceThreadId
    : undefined,
```

`deliveryTarget.deliver` is true only when `isDeliverableMessageChannel(turnSourceChannel)` holds, and that set is `[...CHANNEL_IDS, ...listRegisteredChannelPluginIds()]`. Built-in channels are always present, but an external channel plugin is only present if registered in the process running the followup. `sessionOnlyOriginChannel` covers only gateway-internal channels (webchat/tui/internal). So for an external-plugin turn source that is not in the in-process deliverable set, the followup dispatched the `channel` alone (already preserved via `fallbackChannel`) and dropped `to`/`accountId`/`threadId`. Downstream, `resolveAgentDeliveryPlan` and the gateway `agent` origin resolution then fell back toward the internal/webchat channel.

## Why This Change Was Made

Channel preservation for this exact path already exists (`fallbackChannel = sessionOnlyOriginChannel ?? params.turnSourceChannel`), added so the resumed run inherits `messageProvider`. The recipient fields are part of the same turn-source routing target and should be preserved with it; dropping them is what loses the route.

```ts
// after
channel: deliveryTarget.deliver ? deliveryTarget.channel : fallbackChannel,
to: deliveryTarget.deliver ? deliveryTarget.to : params.turnSourceTo,
accountId: deliveryTarget.deliver ? deliveryTarget.accountId : params.turnSourceAccountId,
threadId: deliveryTarget.deliver ? deliveryTarget.threadId : params.turnSourceThreadId,
```

When the resolved delivery target is used, the normalized values from it are kept (unchanged). Otherwise the raw turn-source fields are forwarded, exactly as the channel already is, so the gateway (the routing authority) can route the post-approval reply back to the originating channel.

## Why this is the right boundary

`buildAgentFollowupArgs` is the single place that maps the captured turn source onto the resume `agent` call. The followup target carries the originating channel/to/accountId/threadId from the exec request (`bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts` / `bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts` build it via `buildExecApprovalFollowupTarget`), so this change preserves data already captured upstream rather than rediscovering it. Built-in channels are unaffected (they take the `deliveryTarget.deliver` branch); gateway-internal channels are unaffected (raw turn-source equals what they forwarded before); sessions with no turn source still forward `undefined`. Both exec hosts share this dispatch path, so the gateway and node host are covered together.

## User Impact

External channel plugins now receive the post-approval agent reply in the originating channel/DM instead of webchat. No config or API change.

## Evidence

- New regression test `preserves the originating routing target for non-built-in plugin channels` in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` fails on pristine `origin/main` (`expected undefined to be 'dm:U1'`) and passes with the patch.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts`: 27 passed (26 existing + new). New case is red on pristine prod, green with the patch.
- `oxfmt --check` and `run-oxlint.mjs` on both changed files: clean.
- `run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`: clean.
- Full `pnpm build` not run (no published-surface/dynamic-import change; OOM-prone box).

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: post-approval session resume for an external-plugin turn source dispatched the gateway followup without the recipient (`to`/`accountId`/`threadId`), so the reply routed to webchat instead of the originating channel.
Real environment tested: drove the real `sendExecApprovalFollowup` production function on pristine `origin/main` (a822c9abaa) and on the patched tree with identical inputs (`turnSourceChannel: "lansenger"`, `turnSourceTo: "dm:U1"`, `turnSourceAccountId: "acct-1"`, `turnSourceThreadId: "thread-9"`), and captured the verbatim argument object the followup hands to the gateway `agent` call. The real `buildAgentFollowupArgs` routing logic and the real deliverable-channel resolution stayed real; only the gateway transport seam (the separate WebSocket service) and the outbound send seam were stubbed.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: invoke `sendExecApprovalFollowup({approvalId:"req-ext", sessionKey:"agent:main:lansenger:dm:U1", turnSourceChannel:"lansenger", turnSourceTo:"dm:U1", turnSourceAccountId:"acct-1", turnSourceThreadId:"thread-9", resultText:"Exec finished (gateway id=req-ext, code 0)\nhello"})` and serialize the captured gateway `agent` arguments.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine origin/main a822c9abaa)
{"channel":"lansenger","deliver":false}
# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs)
{"channel":"lansenger","to":"dm:U1","accountId":"acct-1","threadId":"thread-9","deliver":false}
```
Observed result after fix: the resume now carries the originating channel recipient (`to`/`accountId`/`threadId`) instead of dropping it, so the gateway routes the post-approval reply back to the originating channel rather than webchat.
What was not tested: no live end-to-end round trip through a real external channel plugin and a running gateway (captured at the followup dispatch boundary with the gateway transport stubbed); full `pnpm build` not run.
